headline: Uber expands Women Preferences, Hims & Hers stock skyrockets
summary: Yahoo Finance Senior Reporter Brooke DiPalma and Market Domination host Josh Lipton discuss some of Monday's top trending stories. Ticketmaster parent company Live Nation (LYV) reached a settlement in a monopolization lawsuit with the US Department of Justice (DOJ). Hims & Hers (HIMS) stock skyrocketed on Monday after a big win: the company struck a deal with Novo Nordisk (NVO) to bring GLP-1 drugs Ozempic and Wegovy onto its platform. Uber's (UBER) "Women Preferences" feature is expanding nationwide. To watch more expert insights and analysis on the latest market action, check out more Market Domination.

headline: Live Nation Gets to Keep Ticketmaster in Antitrust Agreement. The Stock Pops.
summary: Live Nation Entertainment  stock climbed on Monday after the company settled an antitrust lawsuit and avoided breaking off its Ticketmaster subsidiary.  The stock was up 6.8% to $166.68, on pace for its highest close since Sept. 17, according to Dow Jones Market Data.  Justice Department lawyers announced the settlement just days after the almost 2-year-old antitrust case went to trial.

headline: Live Nation Reaches Settlement in Federal Antitrust Case
summary: The Justice Department and  Live Nation  have reached a settlement that resolves the government’s monopolization lawsuit against the company, sparing the concerts-and-ticketing giant from the threat of its business being broken up.  The deal would make it easier for other promoters to compete for business at amphitheaters and arenas that Live Nation controls, and would restrict the company’s ability to use exclusive ticketing contracts at venues.  New York Attorney General  Letitia James  said last month that she would continue the case “regardless of the path that the Department of Justice takes.”

raw.headline: Live Nation Reaches Settlement in Federal Antitrust Case
raw.summary: The Justice Department and  Live Nation  have reached a settlement that resolves the government’s monopolization lawsuit against the company, sparing the concerts-and-ticketing giant from the threat of its business being broken up.  The deal would make it easier for other promoters to compete for business at amphitheaters and arenas that Live Nation controls, and would restrict the company’s ability to use exclusive ticketing contracts at venues.  New York Attorney General  Letitia James  said last month that she would continue the case “regardless of the path that the Department of Justice takes.”
